questions.question:
State whether the following statement is True or False with reason for your answer:
Coroutine is just another name for a subroutine.
questions.to_find: Whether a coroutine is the same as a subroutine.

understand.plain: The question asks us to determine if a coroutine and a subroutine are identical concepts. A subroutine is a routine that is called, executes completely, and returns control to the caller. A coroutine is fundamentally different — it can be suspended and resumed, allowing two or more routines to interleave execution without a strict caller-callee hierarchy.

keywords.explain: A block of code that is called from a main program, executes completely, and returns to the caller. Only one activation at a time; has a strict entry and exit point.
keywords.example: A function `int square(int x) { return x*x; }` is a subroutine.

keywords.explain: A generalization of a subroutine that allows multiple entry and exit points. A coroutine can suspend its execution midway and pass control to another coroutine, which can later resume the first coroutine from where it left off.
keywords.example: Producer-consumer implemented as two coroutines that alternate control between each other.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='520' height='200' viewBox='0 0 520 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='220' height='80' class='bg' rx='8'/><text x='120' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Subroutine</text><text x='120' y='58' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Call -> Runs -> Return</text><text x='120' y='78' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Single entry/exit</text><rect x='290' y='10' width='220' height='80' class='hi' rx='8'/><text x='400' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Coroutine</text><text x='400' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Can suspend & resume</text><text x='400' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Multiple entry/exit points</text><text x='260' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>!=</text><text x='120' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Caller controls fully</text><text x='400' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Symmetric: A resumes B, B resumes A</text><text x='260' y='170' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>Coroutines generalize subroutines — NOT the same thing</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two boxes side by side: Subroutine (single entry/exit, caller controls) and Coroutine (multiple entry/exit, symmetric control), separated by a not-equal sign.

terms.meaning: Called by a master routine; runs to completion; returns to caller.
terms.example: A C function called from main.
terms.connects: Represents the simpler, one-way call-return model.

terms.meaning: Can be paused mid-execution and resumed; symmetric relationship between two routines.
terms.example: Producer and consumer coroutines exchanging data iteratively.
terms.connects: Shows that coroutines have capabilities subroutines do not have.

terms.example: In subroutine: CALL/RETURN; in coroutine: RESUME (bidirectional).
terms.connects: The key distinction between the two concepts.

given.plan: Compare the fundamental properties of coroutines and subroutines. If there is any property that one has which the other does not, they are not the same.

steps.title: Properties of a subroutine
steps.apply: A subroutine has a single entry point. When called, it executes from beginning to end and then returns. The caller always waits for the subroutine to complete. The relationship is asymmetric (master-slave).
steps.note: Subroutines have a strict LIFO call stack discipline.

steps.title: Properties of a coroutine
steps.apply: A coroutine can be suspended at any point using a 'resume' or 'yield' operation. When another coroutine resumes it, execution continues from where it was suspended, not from the beginning. Two coroutines can call each other symmetrically — neither is strictly the 'master'. This enables cooperative multitasking patterns like producer-consumer.
steps.note: Coroutines maintain their own execution state (local variables, program counter) between suspensions.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.apply: Since a coroutine can be suspended and resumed multiple times (multiple entry points), while a subroutine always starts from its beginning and runs to completion, they are fundamentally different. Coroutines generalize subroutines. The statement is FALSE.
steps.note: A subroutine is a special (restricted) case of a coroutine — so they are related but not identical.

solution.result: FALSE. Coroutines are NOT the same as subroutines. A coroutine can be suspended and resumed at multiple points, whereas a subroutine always executes from start to finish before returning control.

questions.formula_note: Coroutine vs subroutine: key distinction is multiple entry/exit and symmetric control transfer in coroutines vs single entry and asymmetric call-return in subroutines.

questions.question:
State whether the following statement is True or False with reason for your answer:
A two pass assembler uses its machine opcode table in the first pass of assembly.
questions.to_find: Whether a two-pass assembler uses its opcode table in pass 1 or pass 2.

understand.plain: In a two-pass assembler, the work is divided into two phases. Pass 1 focuses on building the symbol table by scanning labels and computing addresses. Pass 2 uses the symbol table and the opcode table to actually generate machine code. The question is asking which pass uses the opcode (machine instruction) table.

keywords.explain: An assembler that reads the source code twice. Pass 1 builds the symbol/label table; Pass 2 generates the actual object code using that symbol table.
keywords.example: In Pass 1, label 'LOOP' is assigned address 1002. In Pass 2, any branch to LOOP is resolved to 1002.

keywords.explain: A table that maps assembly language mnemonics (like MOV, ADD) to their binary machine code equivalents.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='520' height='200' viewBox='0 0 520 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='40' width='220' height='120' class='bg' rx='8'/><text x='120' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Pass 1</text><text x='120' y='88' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Scan labels</text><text x='120' y='108' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Build Symbol Table</text><text x='120' y='128' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Compute addresses</text><text x='120' y='148' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Uses: opcode table (for size)</text><rect x='290' y='40' width='220' height='120' class='hi' rx='8'/><text x='400' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Pass 2</text><text x='400' y='88' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Translate mnemonics</text><text x='400' y='108' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Use opcode table (primary)</text><text x='400' y='128' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Resolve symbols</text><text x='400' y='148' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Generate object code</text><polygon points='240,100 270,90 270,110' class='ok'/><text x='10' y='195' class='tag mut'>Opcode table primarily used in Pass 2 for code generation</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two boxes: Pass 1 (build symbol table, scan labels) and Pass 2 (use opcode table, generate object code). Arrow from Pass 1 to Pass 2.

given.aim: Identify which pass of a two-pass assembler uses the opcode table.

terms.meaning: Scan for labels/symbols, assign addresses, build symbol table. Also processes directives to determine instruction sizes.
terms.example: Label 'START' at address 1000 is stored in symbol table.
terms.connects: Pass 1 may look up opcode table only to determine instruction length, not to generate code.

terms.meaning: Use symbol table and opcode table to translate each mnemonic to binary and generate object code.
terms.connects: Pass 2 is where the opcode table is primarily and fully used.

terms.meaning: Maps assembly mnemonics to binary opcodes and specifies operand encoding.
terms.connects: Essential for code generation in Pass 2.

given.plan: Recall the tasks performed in each pass of a two-pass assembler to determine when the opcode table is used.

steps.title: What does Pass 1 do?
steps.apply: Pass 1 reads the source program and builds the symbol table. For each label, it records the address. To compute addresses correctly, it needs to know how many bytes each instruction takes. This requires knowing instruction sizes (which may need a partial lookup of the opcode table), but it does NOT generate machine code — it does not need the full opcode encoding.
steps.note: Some assemblers do reference the opcode table in Pass 1 solely for instruction length, not for code generation.

steps.title: What does Pass 2 do?
steps.apply: Pass 2 re-reads the source, looks up each mnemonic in the opcode table to get the binary encoding, replaces symbolic operands with addresses from the symbol table, and outputs the object code. This is the pass where the opcode table is extensively and primarily used.
steps.note: Code generation is exclusively a Pass 2 function.

steps.title: Evaluate the statement
steps.apply: The statement claims the opcode table is used 'in the first pass'. While a limited opcode table reference may occur in Pass 1 (for instruction sizes), the primary use of the machine opcode table is in Pass 2 for translating mnemonics to machine code. The statement as given is considered FALSE in standard assembler theory — the opcode table's main role is in Pass 2.
steps.note: Standard GATE answer: FALSE. The opcode table is used in Pass 2, not Pass 1.

solution.result: FALSE. A two-pass assembler primarily uses the machine opcode table in the second pass (Pass 2) for object code generation, not in the first pass.

questions.formula_note: Two-pass assembler architecture: Pass 1 builds symbol table; Pass 2 generates object code using the opcode table.

questions.question:
State whether the following statement is True or False with reason for your answer:
A subroutine cannot always be used to replace a macro in an assembly language program.
questions.to_find: Whether a subroutine can always substitute for a macro in assembly language.

understand.plain: Macros in assembly language are text-substitution mechanisms that expand inline at each point of use. Subroutines are called routines. While both can perform repeated operations, macros have capabilities that subroutines lack. For example, macros can use conditional assembly directives and can manipulate the code structure itself, which a subroutine cannot replicate through a function call.

keywords.explain: A named block of assembly instructions that is textually substituted at each point it is invoked. Macros can take parameters and use conditional assembly directives.
keywords.example: MACRO SWAP x, y  / MOV temp, x / MOV x, y / MOV y, temp / MEND — each use of SWAP expands inline.

keywords.explain: A block of code that is called via a CALL instruction. The caller passes control (and possibly arguments) and the subroutine returns after execution.
keywords.example: CALL SWAP_ROUTINE — the CPU jumps to SWAP_ROUTINE and returns.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='520' height='210' viewBox='0 0 520 210' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='220' height='140' class='bg' rx='8'/><text x='120' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Macro</text><text x='120' y='58' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Inline expansion</text><text x='120' y='78' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Conditional assembly (IF/ELSE)</text><text x='120' y='98' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Can generate different code</text><text x='120' y='118' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>No CALL overhead</text><text x='120' y='138' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Code at compile time</text><rect x='290' y='10' width='220' height='140' class='hi' rx='8'/><text x='400' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Subroutine</text><text x='400' y='58' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>CALL + RETURN overhead</text><text x='400' y='78' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Single copy of code</text><text x='400' y='98' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>No conditional assembly</text><text x='400' y='118' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Cannot replace macros</text><text x='400' y='138' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>that use IRP/conditional</text><text x='120' y='185' text-anchor='middle' class='tag no'>Macros with conditional assembly CANNOT be replaced by subroutines</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two boxes comparing Macro (inline expansion, conditional assembly, no call overhead) versus Subroutine (call/return overhead, no conditional assembly). Note that macros with conditional assembly cannot be replaced.

given.aim: Determine if subroutines can always replace macros in assembly programs.

terms.meaning: A feature in macros that allows different code to be generated based on parameters evaluated at assembly time (IF, ELSE directives inside macros).
terms.example: IF PARAM EQ 0 / MOV AX, 0 / ELSE / MOV AX, PARAM / ENDIF
terms.connects: This is a macro-only capability that subroutines cannot replicate.

terms.meaning: The macro body is inserted directly at the call site, becoming part of the surrounding code.
terms.example: A macro can insert instructions that alter register usage of surrounding code — not possible with CALL.
terms.connects: Macros can affect code structure; subroutines cannot.

terms.meaning: Macros can repeat code generation for a list of parameters at assembly time.
terms.example: IRP X, <1,2,3> / MOV AX, X / ENDM — generates 3 MOV instructions.
terms.connects: This code generation pattern is impossible to replicate with a runtime subroutine.

given.plan: Identify situations where a macro's behavior cannot be duplicated by a subroutine call to prove the statement TRUE.

steps.title: When subroutines CAN replace macros
steps.apply: For simple repeated code sequences without conditional assembly or special directives, a subroutine is functionally equivalent to a macro (with the difference being code size vs. speed trade-off).
steps.note: Simple macros can always be replaced by subroutines, but complex macros cannot.

steps.title: When subroutines CANNOT replace macros
steps.apply:
1. Macros with conditional assembly (IF/ELSE directives): the macro can generate entirely different code based on arguments — a subroutine must include all branches at runtime. 
2. Macros that insert inline code affecting surrounding instructions (e.g., inline register manipulation). 
3. Macros using IRP (indefinite repeat) that generate variable amounts of code at assembly time. 
4. Macros that define labels or data structures inline cannot be replaced by subroutines.
steps.note: Conditional assembly and code generation at assembly time are the key capabilities that separate macros from subroutines.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.apply: Since there exist cases (conditional assembly, IRP, inline label definitions) where a subroutine cannot replicate the behavior of a macro, the statement 'A subroutine cannot always be used to replace a macro' is TRUE.
steps.note: The reverse is also notable: subroutines save code space; macros may generate larger code but with no call overhead.

solution.result: TRUE. A subroutine cannot always replace a macro because macros support conditional assembly, indefinite repeat (IRP), and inline code generation at assembly time, which subroutines cannot replicate.

questions.formula_note: Macro vs subroutine: macros with conditional assembly directives (IF/ELSE/IRP) cannot be replaced by subroutines — a key distinction in assembler design.

questions.question:
State whether the following statement is True or False with reason for your answer:
A symbol declared as 'external' in an assembly language program is assigned an address outside the program by the assembler itself.
questions.to_find: Whether the assembler or the linker assigns addresses to external symbols.

understand.plain: When a symbol is declared 'external' in an assembly language program, it means the symbol is defined in a different module (another object file). The assembler does not know where this symbol will reside in memory — it cannot assign an address. Instead, the assembler leaves the address unresolved (a placeholder), and the linker resolves the address later when combining all modules.

keywords.explain: A symbol (variable or function label) used in one assembly module but defined in another module. Declared with EXTERN or EXTRN directive.
keywords.example: EXTRN PRINTF — tells the assembler that PRINTF is defined elsewhere.

keywords.explain: A program that combines multiple object files into a single executable and resolves cross-module symbol references by assigning final addresses.
keywords.example: The linker knows PRINTF is at address 0x4001A0 in the C library and fills that into all callers.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='520' height='200' viewBox='0 0 520 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='20' width='140' height='60' class='bg' rx='6'/><text x='80' y='45' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Module A</text><text x='80' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>EXTRN FUNC</text><rect x='10' y='110' width='140' height='60' class='bg' rx='6'/><text x='80' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Assembler</text><text x='80' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Leaves ??? for FUNC</text><rect x='220' y='65' width='140' height='60' class='hi' rx='6'/><text x='290' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Linker</text><text x='290' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Resolves FUNC address</text><rect x='370' y='20' width='140' height='60' class='bg' rx='6'/><text x='440' y='45' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Module B</text><text x='440' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>FUNC defined here</text><polygon points='155,140 210,95 210,85 155,130' class='ok'/><polygon points='365,50 310,85 310,95 365,60' class='ok'/><text x='260' y='185' text-anchor='middle' class='tag no'>Assembler does NOT assign address — Linker does</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Module A declares EXTRN FUNC. Assembler leaves address as unresolved. The Linker combines Module A and Module B (where FUNC is defined) and resolves the address.

given.aim: Determine who assigns addresses to external symbols: the assembler or the linker.

terms.meaning: Translates assembly source to object code. For external symbols, it records them in a relocation table with placeholder addresses.
terms.example: CALL FUNC -> CALL 0x00000000 (placeholder), with a relocation entry for FUNC.
terms.connects: The assembler cannot know the final address since FUNC is in another file.

terms.meaning: Combines object files, resolves external references by looking up symbol definitions across all modules, and assigns final memory addresses.
terms.example: Linker sees FUNC defined in libfoo.obj at offset 0x200 and patches the placeholder.
terms.connects: The linker is the one that actually assigns addresses to external symbols.

terms.meaning: A table in the object file that lists all unresolved external references so the linker knows what to patch.
terms.example: Entry: (offset=0x10, symbol=FUNC, type=CALL) — linker fills in the correct address.
terms.connects: Mechanism by which the assembler communicates external references to the linker.

given.plan: Recall the distinct roles of assembler and linker in resolving external symbols to determine the correct answer.

steps.title: Assembler's handling of external symbols
steps.apply: When the assembler encounters EXTRN SYMBOL, it knows the symbol is defined elsewhere. It generates a placeholder (usually 0 or an offset) in the object code and records the reference in a relocation/external reference table. The assembler does NOT and CANNOT assign an address because it only sees one module at a time.
steps.note: The assembler's scope is limited to a single source file/module.

steps.title: Linker's role in address assignment
steps.apply: The linker takes all object files, finds where each external symbol is actually defined (its address in the defining module), and patches all references to that symbol across all modules. This is called 'link-time address resolution'. The linker assigns the final address to the external symbol.
steps.note: This is the entire purpose of the linker: resolving cross-module references.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.apply: The statement says 'the assembler itself' assigns an address to external symbols. This is FALSE. The assembler leaves the address unresolved; it is the LINKER that assigns the final address to external symbols.
steps.note: Confusing assembler and linker responsibilities is a common conceptual error.

solution.result: FALSE. External symbols are NOT assigned addresses by the assembler. The assembler records them as unresolved references in the object file's relocation table. The LINKER resolves and assigns addresses to external symbols during the linking phase.

questions.formula_note: Compilation toolchain: Assembler -> Object file (with relocation table) -> Linker -> Executable (external symbols resolved). External symbol address assignment is a linker responsibility.

questions.question:
The pass numbers for each of the following activities in a two pass assembler:
i. object code generation
ii. literals added to literal table
iii. listing printed
iv. address resolution of local symbols
respectively are
A. 1,2,1,2
B. 2,1,2,1
C. 2,1,1,2
D. 1,2,2,2
questions.to_find: The pass number (1 or 2) for each of the four listed assembler activities.

understand.plain: In a two-pass assembler, Pass 1 scans the source to build the symbol and literal tables and assign addresses. Pass 2 uses those tables to generate the final object code and produce the listing. We need to assign each activity to the correct pass.

keywords.explain: First scan of source code. Main jobs: assign addresses to labels, build symbol table, add literals to literal table, process certain directives. Does NOT generate object code.
keywords.example: Label LOOP gets address 1004; literal =5 is added to literal table.

keywords.explain: Second scan. Uses symbol table to resolve references, translates mnemonics to opcodes, generates object code, and prints the listing.
keywords.example: MOV AX, [LOOP] -> translates to binary using address of LOOP from symbol table.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='520' height='220' viewBox='0 0 520 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='230' height='170' class='bg' rx='8'/><text x='125' y='38' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Pass 1</text><text x='125' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>- Build Symbol Table</text><text x='125' y='82' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>- Add literals to Literal Table (ii)</text><text x='125' y='102' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>- Address resolution of local symbols (iv)</text><text x='125' y='122' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>- Process ORIGIN/EQU directives</text><text x='125' y='142' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>NO object code generated</text><text x='125' y='162' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>NO listing printed</text><rect x='280' y='10' width='230' height='170' class='hi' rx='8'/><text x='395' y='38' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Pass 2</text><text x='395' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>- Object code generation (i)</text><text x='395' y='82' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>- Listing printed (iii)</text><text x='395' y='102' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>- Resolve external references</text><text x='395' y='122' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>- Assign literal addresses</text><text x='395' y='142' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>- Generate relocatable code</text><text x='260' y='205' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Answer: i->2, ii->1, iii->2, iv->1 => B. 2,1,2,1</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two boxes: Pass 1 (adds literals to literal table, resolves local symbol addresses) and Pass 2 (generates object code, prints listing).

given.aim: Map each assembler activity to the correct pass (1 or 2).

terms.meaning: Translating assembly mnemonics and resolved operands into binary machine code output.
terms.connects: Requires symbol table (built in Pass 1) -> happens in Pass 2.

terms.term: Activity ii: Literals added to literal table
terms.meaning: When a literal (like =5 or ='A') is encountered, it is recorded in a literal table to be assigned an address.
terms.example: MOV AX, =10 -> literal 10 added to literal table in Pass 1.

terms.meaning: The assembly listing (source with addresses and generated code side by side) is printed as output.
terms.connects: Requires object code to be generated, so it's printed in Pass 2.

terms.term: Activity iv: Address resolution of local symbols
terms.meaning: Assigning memory addresses to labels/symbols defined within the program.
terms.example: Label LOOP: gets address 1004 after processing preceding instructions.
terms.connects: This is the primary job of Pass 1.

given.plan: For each activity, determine which pass performs it based on the standard two-pass assembler algorithm.

steps.title: Classify Pass 1 activities
steps.apply: Pass 1 tasks: scanning labels and assigning addresses (activity iv: address resolution of local symbols -> Pass 1), and recording literals in the literal table (activity ii: literals added to literal table -> Pass 1). Pass 1 does not generate object code and does not print the listing.
steps.note: Activities ii and iv are Pass 1 tasks.

steps.title: Classify Pass 2 activities
steps.apply: Pass 2 tasks: using the symbol and literal tables to translate each instruction to object code (activity i: object code generation -> Pass 2), and printing the assembly listing once both source and generated code are available (activity iii: listing printed -> Pass 2).
steps.note: Activities i and iii are Pass 2 tasks.

steps.title: Match to options
steps.apply: i -> Pass 2, ii -> Pass 1, iii -> Pass 2, iv -> Pass 1. The sequence for (i, ii, iii, iv) = (2, 1, 2, 1). This matches option B: 2,1,2,1.
steps.note: This is a classic GATE question; the answer is well-established as B.

solution.result: B. 2,1,2,1 — Object code generation: Pass 2; Literals to literal table: Pass 1; Listing printed: Pass 2; Address resolution of local symbols: Pass 1.

questions.formula_note: Two-pass assembler: Pass 1 builds symbol/literal tables and resolves local addresses; Pass 2 generates object code and the assembly listing.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements about the use of backpatching in a compiler for intermediate code generation:
I. Backpatching can be used to generate code for Boolean expressions in one pass.
II. Backpatching can be used to generate code for flow-of-control statements in one pass.
Which ONE of the following options is CORRECT?
A. Only I is correct
B. Only II is correct
C. Both I and II are correct
D. Neither I nor II is correct
questions.answer: C. Both I and II are correct
questions.to_find: Which of the two statements about backpatching are correct.

understand.plain: Backpatching is a technique used during one-pass intermediate code generation. When a conditional branch instruction is generated but the target label is not yet known (because it refers to code not yet generated), the branch is emitted with a blank/placeholder target. Once the target is known, the compiler 'backpatches' all such placeholders with the correct label. This works for both Boolean expression code generation and flow-of-control (if-else, while) code generation.

keywords.explain: A technique to handle forward references in one-pass code generation. Branch instructions are emitted with empty targets; a list of such incomplete instructions is maintained. When the target label is encountered, all instructions on the list are patched with the correct target.
keywords.example: When generating if (cond) goto L, L is unknown. Store instruction index in truelist/falselist. Later patch with L's address.

keywords.explain: Lists of instruction indices that must be backpatched with the correct target label for true and false branches of a Boolean expression.
keywords.example: For expression A && B, A's falselist and B's falselist are merged; B's truelist becomes the expression's truelist.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='520' height='220' viewBox='0 0 520 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='500' height='50' class='bg' rx='8'/><text x='260' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>One-Pass Code Generation with Backpatching</text><text x='260' y='50' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Forward references handled by maintaining truelist/falselist</text><rect x='10' y='75' width='230' height='65' class='hi' rx='6'/><text x='125' y='98' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Boolean Expressions (I)</text><text x='125' y='118' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Backpatch for A && B, A || B</text><text x='125' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>truelist/falselist mechanism</text><rect x='280' y='75' width='230' height='65' class='hi' rx='6'/><text x='395' y='98' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Flow-of-Control (II)</text><text x='395' y='118' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>if-else, while, for statements</text><text x='395' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Backpatch goto targets</text><rect x='10' y='160' width='500' height='45' class='bg' rx='6'/><text x='260' y='182' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ok'>Both I and II are correct</text><text x='260' y='200' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Backpatching enables single-pass generation for both cases</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Diagram showing one-pass code generation with backpatching applied to both Boolean expressions (truelist/falselist) and flow-of-control statements (if-else, while). Both are valid use cases.

given.aim: Determine whether backpatching supports one-pass code generation for Boolean expressions and/or flow-of-control statements.

terms.meaning: When generating code for expressions like A && B or A || B, branch targets may be forward references. Backpatching maintains truelist and falselist to patch them later in one pass.
terms.example: For A && B: generate 'if A goto _' (truelist of A goes to B's evaluation); if B is true, goto truelist; else goto falselist.
terms.connects: Statement I is about this use case.

terms.meaning: For if-then-else and while statements, the goto targets (e.g., else part, end of loop) are not known when the branch instruction is emitted. Backpatching fills these in once the target code is generated.
terms.example: while (cond) S1: emit 'ifFalse cond goto _'; generate S1; emit 'goto loop_start'; backpatch '_' with address after S1.
terms.connects: Statement II is about this use case.

terms.meaning: Generating intermediate code in a single pass over the source without needing to re-scan. Backpatching makes this possible by deferring target resolution.
terms.example: Without backpatching, two passes would be needed: one to determine labels, one to generate code.
terms.connects: Both statements claim one-pass generation is possible with backpatching.

given.plan: Recall the standard backpatching algorithm (from Dragon Book / compiler theory) and verify whether it applies to Boolean expressions (Statement I) and flow-of-control statements (Statement II).

steps.title: Verify Statement I: Boolean expressions
steps.apply: The classical backpatching technique (Aho, Lam, Sethi, Ullman — Dragon Book) explicitly covers code generation for Boolean expressions in one pass using truelist and falselist. When translating A && B: code for A is generated with a conditional jump; if A is true, we fall through to B's code; the falselist of A is merged with the falselist of B; and B's truelist becomes the overall truelist. All forward branch targets are resolved (backpatched) when the target labels are generated. Statement I is CORRECT.
steps.note: This is one of the original motivations for the backpatching technique.

steps.title: Verify Statement II: Flow-of-control statements
steps.apply: The Dragon Book also explicitly covers backpatching for if-then-else and while statements. For 'if (B) S1 else S2': generate code for B (producing truelist/falselist), backpatch B.truelist to start of S1, generate S1, emit unconditional goto (goes to end of if-else, address unknown yet — store in a list), backpatch B.falselist to start of S2, generate S2, then backpatch the unconditional goto to the next instruction. All done in one pass. Statement II is CORRECT.
steps.note: This is the primary use case described in compiler textbooks for backpatching.

steps.title: Select the correct option
steps.apply: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct. Backpatching enables one-pass intermediate code generation for both Boolean expressions and flow-of-control statements. The answer is option C.
steps.note: This is a standard result from compiler design theory. Both uses of backpatching are well-documented.

solution.result: C. Both I and II are correct. Backpatching can be used to generate code for both Boolean expressions and flow-of-control statements in one pass.

questions.formula_note: Backpatching technique (Dragon Book): maintains truelist/falselist for Boolean expressions and patches forward goto targets for flow-of-control statements — enables single-pass intermediate code generation for both.
